does: This paper investigates why visual grounding models fail when presented with semantically mismatched captions—situations where the described object is not actually present in the image. The authors develop a similarity-controlled counterfactual caption generation protocol that systematically perturbs either object or contextual components of referring expressions while maintaining precise control over embedding similarity levels. Through this controlled perturbation framework, they perform a mechanistic interpretability analysis to examine whether embedding anisotropy (non-uniform variance in different embedding directions) contributes to these counterfactual failures. The work reveals that models exhibit approximation behavior by producing plausible bounding boxes that only partially satisfy expressions, and the analysis identifies embedding anisotropy as a contributing factor to this unreliable behavior.
connects: This work builds on the visual grounding literature, which traditionally evaluates models on benchmarks assuming the described object is always present in the image. It extends recent efforts to evaluate model robustness under distribution shifts and semantic mismatches, drawing from work in adversarial robustness and out-of-distribution evaluation. The paper also connects to growing research on mechanistic interpretability in neural networks, which seeks to understand internal mechanisms causing model failures. The focus on embedding anisotropy specifically relates to prior findings in NLP and vision-language models showing that embeddings often exhibit non-uniform geometric properties that can lead to unexpected behavior. The counterfactual perturbation methodology builds on existing work in generating adversarial and counterfactual examples for understanding model decision boundaries.
enables: This work opens directions for improving visual grounding model reliability by identifying embedding anisotropy as a potential intervention target for enhancing faithfulness and interpretability. The similarity-controlled perturbation protocol provides a reusable framework for systematically testing visual grounding models under controlled semantic mismatches, enabling more rigorous evaluation of model robustness. The mechanistic insights about anisotropy's role in counterfactual failures could inform regularization or training techniques to produce more isotropic embeddings and more trustworthy grounding predictions. The interpretability perspective suggests future work on developing grounding models that remain reliable when descriptions do not match image content, with applications to robustness in real-world scenarios where misalignment between language and visual input frequently occurs. This establishes a foundation for building more interpretable and faithful vision-language systems.
